title: Scale Leadership Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Leadership maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Scale Leadership Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Delegation & Decision Rights, Talent & Coaching, Operating Rhythm, Culture & Psychological Safety. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How often do routine decisions escalate to senior leaders?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Delegation & Decision Rights: How often do routine decisions escalate to senior leaders
conceptHelp.summary: Delegation & Decision Rights means deciding which leaders own which choices so routine decisions do not climb unnecessarily to the founder or executive team. This question narrows the concept to "How often do routine decisions escalate to senior leaders?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Delegation & Decision Rights matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How often do routine decisions escalate to senior leaders?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Delegation & Decision Rights; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Delegation & Decision Rights, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Delegation & Decision Rights has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Delegation & Decision Rights is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How systematic is hiring for critical roles?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Talent & Coaching: How systematic is hiring for critical roles
conceptHelp.summary: Talent & Coaching means hiring, developing, and coaching people so scaling quality does not depend only on senior rescuers. This question narrows the concept to "How systematic is hiring for critical roles?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Talent & Coaching matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.

questions.text: How are conflict and accountability balanced?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How often do leaders let teams make consequential decisions within clear constraints?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows team decisions within leadership constraints; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of team decisions within leadership constraints, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show team decisions within leadership constraints in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Team decisions within leadership constraints is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Delegation & Decision Rights: How often do leaders let teams make consequential decisions within clear constraints
conceptHelp.summary: Delegation & Decision Rights is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How often do leaders let teams make consequential decisions within clear constraints?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Delegation & Decision Rights matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
